text:
```json
[
  "An operating system (OS) is the executive manager of a computing system that manages all hardware and software.",
  "An OS controls every file, device, section of main memory, and nano-second of processing.",
  "An OS includes several managers, such as the Memory manager, Processor manager, Device manager, and File manager.",
  "Each OS manager must work harmoniously with other managers.",
  "The Memory Manager is in charge of main memory (RAM).",
  "The Memory Manager checks the validity of each request for memory space.",
  "The Memory Manager allocates, reallocates, and deallocates memory space.",
  "The Processor Manager decides how to allocate the CPU.",
  "The Processor Manager keeps track of the status of each job, process, and thread.",
  "The Processor Manager monitors the CPU while handling each process’s transition.",
  "The Device Manager is responsible for connecting with every device available on the system.",
  "The Device Manager chooses the most efficient way to allocate devices based on device scheduling policies.",
  "The File Manager keeps track of every file in the system, including data files, program files, utilities, compilers, and applications.",
  "The File Manager enforces restrictions on file access control.",
  "Network Management is a feature for OS with networking capability.",
  "The Network Manager provides a convenient way for authorized users to share resources.",
  "The Network Manager takes overall responsibility for every aspect of network connectivity.",
  "Network connectivity responsibilities include managing devices, files, memory space, CPU capacity, transmission connections, and encryption types.",
  "User interfaces include Graphical User Interface (GUI) and Command Line Interface.",
  "Augusta Ada Byron, the world’s first computer programmer, played a key role in formulating the notion of programming the Analytical Engine.",
  "In 1991, the London Science Museum built the Difference Engine using Babbage's plans, and it worked perfectly.",
  "Hollerith’s punched-card tabulating machines are the predecessors of today’s business machines.",
  "Hollerith invented an electronic punching device.",
  "Hollerith founded Tabulating Machine Co., which later became IBM.",
  "ENIAC was created by Dr. John Mauchly and J. Presper Eckert.",
  "ENIAC was mainly used to solve math problems.",
  "The stored program concept means the computer program and data are stored in the computer’s memory.",
  "The first generation of computers used vacuum tubes.",
  "First generation computers did not work most of the time because vacuum tubes failed frequently.",
  "Eckert and Mauchly delivered the first UNIVAC to the U.S. Census Bureau in 1951.",
  "UNIVAC gained fame by predicting Eisenhower as the winner of the 1952 U.S. presidential election.",
  "UNIVAC was easier to use than ENIAC, had fewer vacuum tubes, and was more reliable.",
  "UNIVAC was a stored program, general-purpose computer that used machine language.",
  "The IBM 701 and IBM 650 were other first-generation computers.",
  "The transistor heralded the second generation of computers.",
  "Second generation computers still used punch cards but also utilized printers, tape storage, and disk storage.",
  "High-level programming languages like COBOL and FORTRAN were introduced in the second generation.",
  "The IBM 1401 was a second-generation computer.",
  "Electronic Recording Machine -- Accounting (ERMA) and American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII) were advancements of the second generation.",
  "The IBM System/360 was a line of compatible computers with an instruction set usable for both business and science.",
  "Third generation computers used timesharing and were accessed remotely by terminals.",
  "The third generation used integrated circuits, resulting in lower costs.",
  "DEC’s minicomputer, the PDP-8, was developed during the third generation.",
  "IBM \"unbundled\" its systems during the third generation.",
  "Wide-area networks (WAN) and Local area networks (LAN) were developed during the third generation.",
  "ARPANET implemented Internet protocols (TCPIP) during the third generation.",
  "Mainframes were proprietary, while minicomputers had open architecture.",
  "Fourth generation computers employed very-large-scale integration (VLSI).",
  "The Intel 4004, the first microprocessor, was developed in the fourth generation.",
  "Apple Computer was founded in the fourth generation.",
  "IBM introduced the Personal Computer (PC) in the fourth generation.",
  "IBM compatibles or clones were introduced in the fourth generation.",
  "The first IBM PC was released in 1981.",
  "Intel provided the microprocessor chip for the first IBM PC.",
  "Microsoft Corporation provided the operating system for the first IBM PC.",
  "The first GUI was developed at Xerox Corporation’s Palo Alto Research Center (PARC).",
  "Macintosh was the first commercial personal computer to offer a user interface.",
  "Microsoft’s Windows 98 is a popular GUI designed for IBM-compatible microcomputers.",
  "Experts forecast that artificial intelligence (AI) will be the hallmark of the fifth generation.",
  "AI, where computers exhibit characteristics of human intelligence, has been slow in coming.",
  "Technologically, we are still in the fourth generation, with engineers focusing on packing more transistors on chips.",
  "This effort to pack more transistors on chips is expected to bring some trappings of AI.",
  "Operating systems are categorized into batch systems, interactive systems, real-time systems, and hybrid systems.",
  "Batch systems were the earliest computers, using punched cards or tapes for input.",
  "In batch systems, jobs are processed serially without user interaction.",
  "The efficiency of batch systems is measured in throughput, which is the number of jobs completed in a period of time.",
  "Interactive systems are also known as time-sharing systems.",
  "Interactive systems are faster than batch systems but slower than real-time systems.",
  "The OS in interactive systems requires time-sharing software to allow users to interact via commands.",
  "Interactive systems provide immediate feedback to the user, with response times in minutes or seconds.",
  "Real-time systems are the fastest of the four OS types.",
  "Real-time systems are used in time-critical environments like space flights, airport traffic control, and sophisticated medical equipment.",
  "Real-time systems must be 100% responsive 100% of the time.",
  "Hybrid systems combine features of batch and interactive systems.",
  "Hybrid systems appear interactive, allowing users to access the system via a terminal and get fast responses.",
  "Hybrid systems accept and run batch programs in the background when the interactive load is light.",
  "Hybrid systems take advantage of free time to execute programs that need no significant operator assistance.",
  "A computer’s hardware is the physical machine and its electronic components.",
  "Main memory is where data and instructions must reside to be processed.",
  "Input/output (I/O) devices are peripheral units such as printers, disk drives, CD drives, and thumb drives.",
  "The Central Processing Unit (CPU) is the brain of a computer.",
  "The CPU controls the operation of the entire computer system.",
  "The CPU consists of circuits (chips) that control the interpretation and execution of instructions.",
  "Current OS features include support for multimedia applications, Internet and Web access, and client/server computing.",
  "Current OS system architecture has been improved through object-oriented design and reorganization of the kernel.",
  "The kernel is the part of the OS that resides in memory at all times.",
  "The introduction of threads is an important improvement in current OS.",
  "A process requires space in main memory and other resources during execution.",
  "A process passes through several states, such as running, waiting, and ready, from its initial arrival to its completion.",
  "In a modern OS, processes are swapped between main memory and secondary storage during execution.",
  "Swapping heavyweight processes incurs high overhead because all process information must be saved to preserve integrity.",
  "A thread is a unit smaller than a process that can be scheduled and executed.",
  "The heavyweight process becomes a passive element that owns resources, while the thread uses the CPU and is scheduled for execution.",
  "Swapping a thread is less time-consuming than swapping a process due to less information needing to be saved.",
  "Multiprocessing is when two or more CPUs share the same main memory, most I/O devices, and control program routines.",
  "In multiprocessing, CPUs service the same job stream and execute distinct processing programs concurrently.",
  "Symmetric multiprocessing allows several independent CPUs to process multiple jobs concurrently.",
  "In symmetric multiprocessing, each CPU has access to the OS and shares memory and secondary storage devices.",
  "In asymmetric multiprocessing, some OS functions are assigned to subordinate processors that take instructions from the main CPU.",
  "The Network Manager coordinates the workings of networked resources in most current OS.",
  "Networked resources coordinated by the Network Manager include shared access to memory space, processors, printers, databases, monitors, and applications.",
  "Cloud Computing changes the role of the OS by accommodating remote access to system resources and providing increased security.",
  "The OS still maintains responsibility for managing all local resources and coordinating data transfer to and from the cloud."
]
```
json_data: An operating system (OS) is the executive manager of a computing system that manages all hardware and software.
An OS controls every file, device, section of main memory, and nano-second of processing.
An OS includes several managers, such as the Memory manager, Processor manager, Device manager, and File manager.
Each OS manager must work harmoniously with other managers.
The Memory Manager is in charge of main memory (RAM).
The Memory Manager checks the validity of each request for memory space.
The Memory Manager allocates, reallocates, and deallocates memory space.
The Processor Manager decides how to allocate the CPU.
The Processor Manager keeps track of the status of each job, process, and thread.
The Processor Manager monitors the CPU while handling each process’s transition.
The Device Manager is responsible for connecting with every device available on the system.
The Device Manager chooses the most efficient way to allocate devices based on device scheduling policies.
The File Manager keeps track of every file in the system, including data files, program files, utilities, compilers, and applications.
The File Manager enforces restrictions on file access control.
Network Management is a feature for OS with networking capability.
The Network Manager provides a convenient way for authorized users to share resources.
The Network Manager takes overall responsibility for every aspect of network connectivity.
Network connectivity responsibilities include managing devices, files, memory space, CPU capacity, transmission connections, and encryption types.
User interfaces include Graphical User Interface (GUI) and Command Line Interface.
Augusta Ada Byron, the world’s first computer programmer, played a key role in formulating the notion of programming the Analytical Engine.
In 1991, the London Science Museum built the Difference Engine using Babbage's plans, and it worked perfectly.
Hollerith’s punched-card tabulating machines are the predecessors of today’s business machines.
Hollerith invented an electronic punching device.
Hollerith founded Tabulating Machine Co., which later became IBM.
ENIAC was created by Dr. John Mauchly and J. Presper Eckert.
ENIAC was mainly used to solve math problems.
The stored program concept means the computer program and data are stored in the computer’s memory.
The first generation of computers used vacuum tubes.
First generation computers did not work most of the time because vacuum tubes failed frequently.
Eckert and Mauchly delivered the first UNIVAC to the U.S. Census Bureau in 1951.
UNIVAC gained fame by predicting Eisenhower as the winner of the 1952 U.S. presidential election.
UNIVAC was easier to use than ENIAC, had fewer vacuum tubes, and was more reliable.
UNIVAC was a stored program, general-purpose computer that used machine language.
The IBM 701 and IBM 650 were other first-generation computers.
The transistor heralded the second generation of computers.
Second generation computers still used punch cards but also utilized printers, tape storage, and disk storage.
High-level programming languages like COBOL and FORTRAN were introduced in the second generation.
The IBM 1401 was a second-generation computer.
Electronic Recording Machine -- Accounting (ERMA) and American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII) were advancements of the second generation.
The IBM System/360 was a line of compatible computers with an instruction set usable for both business and science.
Third generation computers used timesharing and were accessed remotely by terminals.
The third generation used integrated circuits, resulting in lower costs.
DEC’s minicomputer, the PDP-8, was developed during the third generation.
IBM "unbundled" its systems during the third generation.
Wide-area networks (WAN) and Local area networks (LAN) were developed during the third generation.
ARPANET implemented Internet protocols (TCPIP) during the third generation.
Mainframes were proprietary, while minicomputers had open architecture.
Fourth generation computers employed very-large-scale integration (VLSI).
The Intel 4004, the first microprocessor, was developed in the fourth generation.
Apple Computer was founded in the fourth generation.
IBM introduced the Personal Computer (PC) in the fourth generation.
IBM compatibles or clones were introduced in the fourth generation.
The first IBM PC was released in 1981.
Intel provided the microprocessor chip for the first IBM PC.
Microsoft Corporation provided the operating system for the first IBM PC.
The first GUI was developed at Xerox Corporation’s Palo Alto Research Center (PARC).
Macintosh was the first commercial personal computer to offer a user interface.
Microsoft’s Windows 98 is a popular GUI designed for IBM-compatible microcomputers.
Experts forecast that artificial intelligence (AI) will be the hallmark of the fifth generation.
AI, where computers exhibit characteristics of human intelligence, has been slow in coming.
Technologically, we are still in the fourth generation, with engineers focusing on packing more transistors on chips.
This effort to pack more transistors on chips is expected to bring some trappings of AI.
Operating systems are categorized into batch systems, interactive systems, real-time systems, and hybrid systems.
Batch systems were the earliest computers, using punched cards or tapes for input.
In batch systems, jobs are processed serially without user interaction.
The efficiency of batch systems is measured in throughput, which is the number of jobs completed in a period of time.
Interactive systems are also known as time-sharing systems.
Interactive systems are faster than batch systems but slower than real-time systems.
The OS in interactive systems requires time-sharing software to allow users to interact via commands.
Interactive systems provide immediate feedback to the user, with response times in minutes or seconds.
Real-time systems are the fastest of the four OS types.
Real-time systems are used in time-critical environments like space flights, airport traffic control, and sophisticated medical equipment.
Real-time systems must be 100% responsive 100% of the time.
Hybrid systems combine features of batch and interactive systems.
Hybrid systems appear interactive, allowing users to access the system via a terminal and get fast responses.
Hybrid systems accept and run batch programs in the background when the interactive load is light.
Hybrid systems take advantage of free time to execute programs that need no significant operator assistance.
A computer’s hardware is the physical machine and its electronic components.
Main memory is where data and instructions must reside to be processed.
Input/output (I/O) devices are peripheral units such as printers, disk drives, CD drives, and thumb drives.
The Central Processing Unit (CPU) is the brain of a computer.
The CPU controls the operation of the entire computer system.
The CPU consists of circuits (chips) that control the interpretation and execution of instructions.
Current OS features include support for multimedia applications, Internet and Web access, and client/server computing.
Current OS system architecture has been improved through object-oriented design and reorganization of the kernel.
The kernel is the part of the OS that resides in memory at all times.
The introduction of threads is an important improvement in current OS.
A process requires space in main memory and other resources during execution.
A process passes through several states, such as running, waiting, and ready, from its initial arrival to its completion.
In a modern OS, processes are swapped between main memory and secondary storage during execution.
Swapping heavyweight processes incurs high overhead because all process information must be saved to preserve integrity.
A thread is a unit smaller than a process that can be scheduled and executed.
The heavyweight process becomes a passive element that owns resources, while the thread uses the CPU and is scheduled for execution.
Swapping a thread is less time-consuming than swapping a process due to less information needing to be saved.
Multiprocessing is when two or more CPUs share the same main memory, most I/O devices, and control program routines.
In multiprocessing, CPUs service the same job stream and execute distinct processing programs concurrently.
Symmetric multiprocessing allows several independent CPUs to process multiple jobs concurrently.
In symmetric multiprocessing, each CPU has access to the OS and shares memory and secondary storage devices.
In asymmetric multiprocessing, some OS functions are assigned to subordinate processors that take instructions from the main CPU.
The Network Manager coordinates the workings of networked resources in most current OS.
Networked resources coordinated by the Network Manager include shared access to memory space, processors, printers, databases, monitors, and applications.
Cloud Computing changes the role of the OS by accommodating remote access to system resources and providing increased security.
The OS still maintains responsibility for managing all local resources and coordinating data transfer to and from the cloud.
